text: The gallbladder is visibly distended and filled with numerous stones, with the outlines of the stones clearly visible due to the lack of bile. The gallbladder appears difficult to retract.
The surgeon uses a clip applier inserted through the patient's left upper quadrant as a liver retractor, pulling the gallbladder away from the liver to better expose Calot's triangle.
The surgeon attempts to dissect the cystic duct and cystic artery, with a clear view of the Calot's triangle, using robotic instruments to carefully separate these structures from surrounding tissue.
The surgeon continues with the infundibular approach, carefully skeletonizing the cystic duct to expose its structure.
The surgeon separates the cystic artery, with the clip applier being used to retract the gallbladder. The tension on the gallbladder is visibly noticeable during this maneuver.

The surgeon addresses a small laceration on the liver, controlling the bleeding, and proceeds to take down the medial side of the gallbladder using the 'open book technique,' which involves spreading the gallbladder apart similar to opening a book.
So it's easier to read the words on a page by opening the medial side of the gallbladder. It's the,

The surgeon exposes the medial side of the gallbladder using the open book technique, enhancing the view of the triangle of Calot for safer dissection. The cystic duct and artery are pulled away from critical structures, including the right hepatic artery and common hepatic duct. An accessory bile duct is visible near the gallbladder, but its risk of injury is reduced by the gallbladder's repositioning.
The surgeon has achieved the critical view of safety and proceeds to remove the gallbladder from the liver bed, ensuring careful handling to avoid injury to the nearby accessory bile duct.
The surgeon uses a clip applier to secure the cystic artery, ensuring that only two structures remain attached to the common bile duct.

The surgeon uses bipolar cautery on one end of the cystic artery and then employs the hook's cut function to divide the artery, optimizing efficiency while awaiting the clip of wire from the bedside assistant.
The surgeon inspects the surgical field for bile leaks, carefully examining the accessory bile duct to ensure it has not been injured during the procedure.
The surgeon attempts to reapply a clip to the cystic artery after it has fallen off, trying to secure it without removing it from the surgical field. The initial attempt is unsuccessful.
But I've done it before, so I know it's doable, so I'll continue to try. I believe this also saves the cost of one clip firing.

The surgeon attempts to place a third clip on the cystic duct, ensuring it is positioned away from the previously placed clips. Following successful clipping, electrocautery is used to divide the cystic duct. The gallbladder is then placed into an endocatch bag for removal.
The surgeon enlarges the fascia at the umbilicus to facilitate the removal of the gallbladder, performing a Hasson cut-down technique to easily widen the incision.
